edges.evidence_phrase: Scanning electron microscopy was used to qualitatively assess surface roughness (SEM, Zeiss Auriga, Carl Zeiss, Germany), and confocal microscopy was used for quantitative assessment (LSM 980 Laser Scanning Microscope, Germany) with a 20x objective and a total measurement area of 790 um x 790 um (n=3). A moving average Gaussian filter with a 30 um cut-off wavelength was used to calculate the arithmetic mean height of the scale-limited surface (Sa). Three samples were used for each surface type to calculate the mean surface roughness at six different areas. A Ram-Hart goniometer was used to measure the sessile drop contact angle to determine surface hydrophilicity (Model 100-25a, Rame-Hart Instrument Co., Succasunna, NJ). 5 uL drops of deionized water were used to measure surface hydrophilicity at six locations per sample (n=3). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a PhI5000 VersaProbe spectrometer (ULVAC-PHI, Inc.) to determine the oxide layer composition. A focused scanning monochromatic Al-Ka source (1,486.6 eV) with a spot size of 200 um was used to acquire spectra, utilizing a base pressure of 1x10 -7  Pa. FAT analyzer mode was used to run the instrument. Detail spectra of 187.85 eV and 46.95 eV were used for the pass energy for survey scans. CasaXPS was utilized for data analysis. Shirley background subtraction was used to integrate the signals. Measurements were taken on each sample in three separate areas.

Biomaterial surface modifications like roughness and wettability can dictate the recruitment and behavior of immune cells at the implant-tissue interface. Previously, we and others have shown that innate immune cells, including neutrophils and macrophages, activate and polarize in response to differences in Ti physicochemical cues in vitro and in vivo [22,25,32,34,35]. Additionally, we have shown that T cell polarization in vitro and in vivo also responds to changes in biomaterial surface roughness and wettability through interactions with macrophages [21,25,27]. This study demonstrates that environmental factors such as obesity significantly impact the inflammatory response and polarization of immune cells within the peri-implant microenvironment. Our data shows that neutrophils, macrophages, T cells, and MSC recruitment are significantly affected by obesity. Here, we utilized rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti implants to demonstrate that obesity attenuates the anti-inflammatory and pro-osseointegrative response associated with increasing surface wettability on modified Ti implants.

Scanning electron microscopy was used to qualitatively assess surface roughness (SEM, Zeiss Auriga, Carl Zeiss, Germany), and confocal microscopy was used for quantitative assessment (LSM 980 Laser Scanning Microscope, Germany) with a 20x objective and a total measurement area of 790 um x 790 um (n=3). A moving average Gaussian filter with a 30 um cut-off wavelength was used to calculate the arithmetic mean height of the scale-limited surface (Sa). Three samples were used for each surface type to calculate the mean surface roughness at six different areas. A Ram-Hart goniometer was used to measure the sessile drop contact angle to determine surface hydrophilicity (Model 100-25a, Rame-Hart Instrument Co., Succasunna, NJ). 5 uL drops of deionized water were used to measure surface hydrophilicity at six locations per sample (n=3). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a PhI5000 VersaProbe spectrometer (ULVAC-PHI, Inc.) to determine the oxide layer composition. A focused scanning monochromatic Al-Ka source (1,486.6 eV) with a spot size of 200 um was used to acquire spectra, utilizing a base pressure of 1x10 -7  Pa. FAT analyzer mode was used to run the instrument. Detail spectra of 187.85 eV and 46.95 eV were used for the pass energy for survey scans. CasaXPS was utilized for data analysis. Shirley background subtraction was used to integrate the signals. Measurements were taken on each sample in three separate areas.

Obesity is a metabolic disease resulting from excess body fat accumulation associated with chronic systemic inflammation. Obesity has been shown to impact the function and activity of neutrophils, macrophages, and T cells, contributing to higher circulating levels of proinflammatory cytokines. Biomaterial surface properties such as roughness and hydrophilicity can influence the behavior of immune cells in the peri-implant microenvironment. This study aimed to determine how obesity induced by a high-fat diet (HFD) affects the inflammatory response to modified titanium (Ti) implants and subsequent bone formation. Obese mice had significantly more neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells and fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the peri-implant tissue than lean mice. Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.

Obesity has been shown to impact the function and activity of neutrophils, macrophages, and T cells, contributing to higher circulating levels of proinflammatory cytokines. Biomaterial surface properties such as roughness and hydrophilicity can influence the behavior of immune cells in the peri-implant microenvironment. This study aimed to determine how obesity induced by a high-fat diet (HFD) affects the inflammatory response to modified titanium (Ti) implants and subsequent bone formation. Obese mice had significantly more neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells and fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the peri-implant tissue than lean mice. Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.

edges.evidence_phrase: Neutrophils and macrophages are myeloid-derived cells that clear wound debris via phagocytosis and initiate the immune response [24,25]. Neutrophils release inflammatory mediators like myeloperoxidase (MPO), neutrophil elastase, and neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs). Macrophages differentiate into pro- or anti-inflammatory macrophages depending on the biochemical and physical cues in the inflammatory microenvironment [25,26]. Our group has demonstrated that macrophage polarization is required to facilitate the amplification and resolution of the inflammatory response to implanted metallic biomaterials and orchestrate the recruitment of T cells and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) using titanium (Ti) as a model [22,25]. Ablating macrophages during the inflammatory response to Ti implants significantly attenuates T cell and MSC recruitment, compromising tissue regeneration [22].

edges.evidence_phrase: Biomaterial surface modifications like roughness and wettability can dictate the recruitment and behavior of immune cells at the implant-tissue interface. Previously, we and others have shown that innate immune cells, including neutrophils and macrophages, activate and polarize in response to differences in Ti physicochemical cues in vitro and in vivo [22,25,32,34,35]. Additionally, we have shown that T cell polarization in vitro and in vivo also responds to changes in biomaterial surface roughness and wettability through interactions with macrophages [21,25,27]. This study demonstrates that environmental factors such as obesity significantly impact the inflammatory response and polarization of immune cells within the peri-implant microenvironment. Our data shows that neutrophils, macrophages, T cells, and MSC recruitment are significantly affected by obesity. Here, we utilized rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti implants to demonstrate that obesity attenuates the anti-inflammatory and pro-osseointegrative response associated with increasing surface wettability on modified Ti implants.

edges.evidence_phrase: Obesity is a metabolic disease resulting from excess body fat accumulation associated with chronic systemic inflammation. Obesity has been shown to impact the function and activity of neutrophils, macrophages, and T cells, contributing to higher circulating levels of proinflammatory cytokines. Biomaterial surface properties such as roughness and hydrophilicity can influence the behavior of immune cells in the peri-implant microenvironment. This study aimed to determine how obesity induced by a high-fat diet (HFD) affects the inflammatory response to modified titanium (Ti) implants and subsequent bone formation. Obese mice had significantly more neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells and fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the peri-implant tissue than lean mice. Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.

edges.evidence_phrase: Biomaterial surface modifications like roughness and wettability can dictate the recruitment and behavior of immune cells at the implant-tissue interface. Previously, we and others have shown that innate immune cells, including neutrophils and macrophages, activate and polarize in response to differences in Ti physicochemical cues in vitro and in vivo [22,25,32,34,35]. Additionally, we have shown that T cell polarization in vitro and in vivo also responds to changes in biomaterial surface roughness and wettability through interactions with macrophages [21,25,27]. This study demonstrates that environmental factors such as obesity significantly impact the inflammatory response and polarization of immune cells within the peri-implant microenvironment. Our data shows that neutrophils, macrophages, T cells, and MSC recruitment are significantly affected by obesity. Here, we utilized rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti implants to demonstrate that obesity attenuates the anti-inflammatory and pro-osseointegrative response associated with increasing surface wettability on modified Ti implants.

edges.evidence_phrase: Biomaterial surface modifications like roughness and wettability can dictate the recruitment and behavior of immune cells at the implant-tissue interface. Previously, we and others have shown that innate immune cells, including neutrophils and macrophages, activate and polarize in response to differences in Ti physicochemical cues in vitro and in vivo [22,25,32,34,35]. Additionally, we have shown that T cell polarization in vitro and in vivo also responds to changes in biomaterial surface roughness and wettability through interactions with macrophages [21,25,27]. This study demonstrates that environmental factors such as obesity significantly impact the inflammatory response and polarization of immune cells within the peri-implant microenvironment. Our data shows that neutrophils, macrophages, T cells, and MSC recruitment are significantly affected by obesity. Here, we utilized rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti implants to demonstrate that obesity attenuates the anti-inflammatory and pro-osseointegrative response associated with increasing surface wettability on modified Ti implants.
